q: Thermodynamics is the study of systems involving energy in the form of heat and work.
A gas confined by a piston in a cylinder that expands upon heating is demonstrating how a thermodynamic system can do work.
When two systems are in thermal equilibrium, there is a continuous net heat transfer between them to maintain the state.
Systems at the same temperature will be in thermal equilibrium with each other.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics states that when two objects are separately in thermodynamic equilibrium with a third object, they all are in thermodynamic equilibrium with each other.
The first law of thermodynamics is an application of the conservation energy for heat and thermodynamic processes.
In the equation $\Delta U = Q - W$, the value of $Q$ is positive when heat is removed from the system.
$W$ is positive if work is done by the system, and negative if work is done on the system.
The internal energy $U$ of a system is defined as the sum of the energies of all the constituent particles.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on volume and pressure, making temperature irrelevant to its value.
The internal energy of an ideal monatomic gas is given as $U = \frac{3}{2} n R T$.
Internal energy of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the absolute temperature.
Heat transfer and work are both energy in transit and neither is stored as such in a system.
Internal energy is a form of energy completely identical to either heat or work because both can change the internal energy of a system.
A thermodynamic process may be defined as a change from one equilibrium state of a system to another.
During a thermodynamic process, a system starting from an initial state passes through various quasistatic states to a final state.
In thermodynamics, a quasi-static process is a thermodynamic process that happens rapidly enough to bypass internal physical thermodynamic equilibrium.
An isobaric process occurs at constant pressure.
An example of an isobaric process is a gas, being slowly heated or cooled, confined by a piston in a cylinder.
The heat $Q$, the work $W$, or the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ are strictly zero in an isobaric process.
In an isobaric process the pressure is constant and the work is equal to the area of the shaded rectangle in a pressure vs volume graph.
An isochoric process occurs at constant pressure and varying volume.
When the volume of a thermodynamic system is constant, no work is done by the system because $W = p \Delta V = 0$.
According to the first law, the change in internal energy must equal any heat flowing into or out of the system in an isochoric process.
For an ideal monatomic gas, the molar heat capacity at constant volume is mathematically expressed as $C_V = \frac{5}{2} R$.
An isothermal process occurs at a constant temperature.
For a process to be isothermal, any heat flow into or out of the system must occur slowly to maintain the thermodynamic equilibrium of the system.
For an ideal gas, internal energy $U$ is a function of both temperature and pressure, ensuring $\Delta U$ remains non-zero in an isothermal process.
Since $\Delta U = 0$, according to the first law, $W = Q$ for an isothermal process.
If the system does the work, the energy comes from heat flowing into the system from the reservoir.
In an adiabatic process, there is continuous heat transfer into or out of the system.
An adiabatic process can be done either by enclosing the system in a thermal insulator or by carrying out the process very quickly.
The first law of thermodynamics $\Delta U = Q - W$ is reduced to $\Delta U = -W$ in an adiabatic process.
For an ideal gas, the work done in an adiabatic process can be calculated as $W = \frac{3}{2} n R (T_i - T_f)$.
Heat engine is a system that converts mechanical energy directly into heat, which is then stored in a reservoir.
A heat engine operates continuously and only heat and work may pass across its boundaries.
A series of processes in which a system is brought back to its initial state is called a cycle.
The thermal efficiency of a heat engine is definitively the ratio of heat input to work output.
Applying the first law of thermodynamic to one complete cycle, remembering that there is no net change of internal energy, we get $Q_H - Q_C = W$.
The transformation of heat into work is usually accomplished in practice by internal combustion engines and steam engines.
A gasoline engine generates the input heat by the combustion of fuel outside the engine cylinders.
In the gasoline engines, the combustion of the gasoline and air takes place explosively by an electric spark.
The gasoline engine cycle involves the performance of six processes, of which four require motion of the piston and are called strokes.
During the compression stroke, the mixture of gasoline vapour and air is expanded until its pressure drops considerably.
Combustion of the hot mixture is caused to take place very rapidly by an electric spark while the volume remains unchanged.
In the power stroke, the hot combustion products expand and push the piston out, thus suffering a drop in pressure and temperature.
An exhaust valve allows some gas to escape until the pressure drops to that of the atmosphere.
The theoretical efficiency of a gasoline engine is solely dependent on its internal temperature and unaffected by the compression ratio.
The diesel engine induces only air into the combustion chamber on its intake stroke.
In a diesel engine, diesel fuel requires an electric spark to ignite because the heat and pressure inside the cylinder are insufficient.
